coredata.dc:title: Deep learning from three-dimensional lithium-ion battery multiphysics model part I: Data development
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Yiheng
Wang, Yun
Niu, Zhiqiang
coredata.dc:description: Fast growing demands for electric vehicles require better longevity, safety and reliability for next-generation high-energy battery technologies. A data-centered battery management system is thus desired to interpret complex battery data and make decisions for properly managing multi-physics battery dynamics. Nowadays, Battery informatics are emerging as promising solutions by leveraging advanced machine learning tools to deliver accurate prediction of battery performance, health and safety, but is hurdled by a scarcity of data. To mitigate this issue, this study presents one of the first studies for data development through both experimental studies and three-dimensional (3-D) multi-physics modeling to underpin a deep learning framework with in-depth examination for battery performance and thermal risk prediction. Specifically, Part I focused on the development of the battery model which was thoroughly validated and analyzed to guarantee the model accuracy by two steps: firstly, we validated the multi-physics model against two commercial Lithium-ion batteries, i.e., Panasonic NCR18650B and 18650BD; Then, the coupling between thermal and electrochemical battery behaviors were analyzed deeply to demonstrate insights obtained from the model, such as voltage evolution and maximum local temperature (hot spot). The developed model proves to be capable of providing insightful and reliable data for the training of convolutional neural network and long short-term memory (CNN-LSTM) in part II.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion batteries
Data development
Multi-physics modeling
Hot spot
Machine learning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of cooling heat transfer coefficient

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The modeling framework for battery informatics. (a) Schematic of Li-ion battery, structures, and operating principles; (b) The Panasonic NCR18650B battery; (c) Li-ion battery geometry and computational domain; (d) Schematics of a ML model for battery performance prediction.
Experimental validation of the multi-physics battery model against the experimental curves [29] for Panasonic NCR18650BD.
Experimental validation of the physics-based model for Panasonic NCR18650B.
Local temperature distribution for Panasonic NCR18650BD battery operating at 2C rate, anisotropic thermal conductivities of 1&10 W m-1K-1, h = 20 W m-2K-1 and Tamb = 22 °C.
3-D temperature distributions in Panasonic NCR18650BD with the thermal conductivities of: (a) 0.1&1 W/m K; (b) 9&90 W m-1K-1 for 2 C rate, h = 20 W m-2K-1 and Tamb = 22 °C.
Performance and maximum temperature (hot spot) of Panasonic NCR18650BD under different thermal conductivities for 2 C rate, h = 20 W m-1K-1and T amb = 22 °C.

Voltage and maximum temperature (hot spot) of Panasonic NCR18650BD operating at room temperature for different convective heat transfer coefficients. Other operating parameters are k = 1&10 W m-1K-1, T amb = 22 °C, and 2 C discharge rate.
Battery voltage and maximum temperature (hot spot) in Panasonic NCR18650BD operating at h = 20 W m-2K-1 and 2 C discharge rate for different ambient temperatures.
Geometrical and operating parameters of Li-ion batteries in this study [29].

row.entry: Density of negative / positive electrode (kg cm-3)
Specific heat of jelly roll (J kg-1K-1)
Electrical conductivity of negative / positive electrode (S m-1)
Diffusion coefficient of negative / positive electrode – solid phase (cm2 s-1)
Mass fraction of active material of negative / positive electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Data development by the 3-D physics-based model for ML.

head.ce:title: Deep learning from three-dimensional lithium-ion battery multiphysics model part I: Data development

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Renewable Energy Resources Lab (RERL), Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, University of California, Irvine, CA, 92697-3975, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aRenewable Energy Resources Lab (RERL), Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, University of California, Irvine, CA, 92697-3975, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Aeronautical and Automotive Engineering, Loughborough University, United Kingdom

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Aeronautical and Automotive Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Aeronautical and Automotive Engineering, Loughborough University, United Kingdom

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fast growing demands for electric vehicles require better longevity, safety and reliability for next-generation high-energy battery technologies. A data-centered battery management system is thus desired to interpret complex battery data and make decisions for properly managing multi-physics battery dynamics. Nowadays, Battery informatics are emerging as promising solutions by leveraging advanced machine learning tools to deliver accurate prediction of battery performance, health and safety, but is hurdled by a scarcity of data. To mitigate this issue, this study presents one of the first studies for data development through both experimental studies and three-dimensional (3-D) multi-physics modeling to underpin a deep learning framework with in-depth examination for battery performance and thermal risk prediction. Specifically, Part I focused on the development of the battery model which was thoroughly validated and analyzed to guarantee the model accuracy by two steps: firstly, we validated the multi-physics model against two commercial Lithium-ion batteries, i.e., Panasonic NCR18650B and 18650BD; Then, the coupling between thermal and electrochemical battery behaviors were analyzed deeply to demonstrate insights obtained from the model, such as voltage evolution and maximum local temperature (hot spot). The developed model proves to be capable of providing insightful and reliable data for the training of convolutional neural network and long short-term memory (CNN-LSTM) in part II.

ce:section.ce:para: Getting on track with the net zero society requires transport emissions to fall by 25 % to around 6 Gt by 2030 [1]. Vehicle electrification has been playing a paramount role to decarbonizing ground transportation sector. The unique demands of automotive applications pose stringent technical requirements on future Lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery design and manufacturing, e.g., calendar life (10 years), cycle life (1000 cycles), temperature range (-30 to 52 °C) and cost ($100/kWh) [2]. Intelligent management of battery dynamics has proved to be one of the effective solutions to delivering efficient, durable, and safe Li-ion batteries [3,4].

Accurate prediction of battery performance and health is a key enabler for by intelligent battery management systems (BMS) but remains challenging due to the complex underlying battery physicochemical correlations [5–8]. Long efforts have been dedicated to developing multi-physics, multi-scale numerical battery models to reveal battery dynamics by iteratively resolving a series of governing equations for charge and heat transfer, along with the variation of operating conditions [9–12]. These numerical battery models significantly benefit the digital design and optimization of battery components, cells and even large-scale packs by saving expensive costs of physical prototypes needed and customizing strategies for battery management, but these model predictive management is low efficiency due to high computational cost, especially for real-time monitoring and control. Apart from that, multi-physics battery models are limited to engaging with real-time data and thus block the potential application of the deep fusion of data sensing and physical models [13]. In addition, a multi-dimensional framework, such as two or three dimensional (2D or 3D) models, is essential to capture local high temperature (hot spot) or other features (e.g. maximum reaction rate and lowest reactant contents) inside a battery. Such multi-dimensional models can be expensive in computation cost in implementation due to complex transport and electrochemistry for extensive study.

Recent battery informatics have shown promise for efficiently predicting various battery behaviors, ensuring battery safety and efficiency by applying data-driven machine learning (ML) methods to extract the features and correlations underlying the battery data from sensors and diagnostics tools [15–17]. To date, the development of battery informatics has been underpinned various data-driven studies regarding battery performance, degradation and safety. For instance, the state of charge (SOC) of Li-ion batteries was accurately predicted by Li et al. [18] via a data-driven convolutional neural network with long short-term memory (CNN-LSTM), evidenced by a low root mean square error (RMSE) less than 0.31 %. Likewise, Severson et al. [19] predicted the battery lifetime by a data-driven ML model, achieving the best prediction accuracy of 9.1 % for cycle life based on the training dataset consisting of 124 commercial lithium iron phosphate/graphite cells. Furthermore, Zhao et al. [20]estimated the battery failure in real-world applications by applying a portfolio of ML tools, e.g., support vector machine, gradient boosting decision tree, etc., which only exhibited an error of 7.7 % for classification by learning 94 pairs of real failure/positive and safety/negative data. The developed model is promising to play as a framework that can enhance the establishment of digital twins for battery systems. These ML models were mainly driven by real battery data and thus their generalization heavily depends on the data size and quality [21,22]. Unfortunately, obtaining high-quality and high-volume real battery data remains scarce due to prohibitive testing cost, especially when large MLs are considered [23].

Alternatively, ongoing efforts has leveraged multi-physics battery modeling to augment training data for battery informatics [24–28]. Typically, physics-based battery models are operated under various conditions to generate massive numerical data which are then used as training dataset for ML training. High-fidelity data derived from 3D models are critical for real-time estimation and the advancement of AI-enabled BMS in electric vehicles. These objectives cannot be fully achieved through experiments alone due to various limitations, such as prohibitive costs. For example, Li et al. [24] developed a Pseudo-2-Dimensional (P2D) model for data development of various MLs, e.g., random forests, artificial neural networks and extreme gradient boosting, eventually accurately estimating the battery SOC with the error 0.358 %; Park et al. [25] employed a P2D model for a Li-Fe battery to generate thermal and electrochemical data for the training of a deep neural network which could significantly accelerate battery performance prediction by 3 orders. Notably, P2D models are high-fidelity but low computational efficiency. Recently, Yu et al. [26] proposed a physics-informed deep learning framework, which integrated a simplified battery electrochemical model and deep neural networks, to accelerate the data preparation and significantly enhanced the accuracy, robustness and generalization of data-driven MLs when predicting battery SOC. However, the ultimate ML accuracy is no doubt largely influenced by that of multi-physics models. Hence, thorough validation of multi-physics battery model must be unscored for sure to ensure the reliability of data-driven MLs based on numerical data.

In this work, we addressed two crucial issues for battery informatics as described above, i.e., data scarcity and data reliability, via three themes: 1) Thorough validation of a 3-D multi-physics Li-ion battery model against multiple battery experimental data, including I-V curves and temperature evolution; 2) Establishment of a diverse dataset which covers a series of operating conditions including as the heat transfer coefficient of surface cooling, discharging rate, coolant temperature, and battery anisotropic thermal conductivities; 3) Verification of the effectiveness of the data development by developing a data-driven CNN-LSTM model. This study mainly focused on the theme 1 and the others were introduced in Part II of this work. These results demonstrated the power of integrating 3-D multi-physics-driven data development and data-driven MLs to deliver robust and accurate surrogate models for future complex battery systems.

Li-ion batteries are rechargeable electrochemical cells that store energy by the transfer of lithium ions between two electrodes. A typical Li-ion battery generally consists of positive and negative electrodes, as well as a separator, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 (a) and photography in Fig. 1(b). The positive electrode typically consists of a lithium metal oxide, while the negative electrode is usually made of graphite or a lithium metal alloy. The electrolyte, which provides an ionic pathway between the electrodes, is a solution of lithium salt in an organic solvent. The separator is a porous membrane and an electronic insulator that prevents direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes. During discharging, lithium ions are released from the negative electrode and travel through the separator, while the electrons travel through the external circuit towards the positive electrode, where they are absorbed into the positive electrode material. The flow of electrons from the anode to the cathode through the external circuit produces electrical current and hence electricity to power external loads.

The study focuses on two of two commercial Li-ion batteries: Panasonic NCR18650B and 18650BD [29]. These batteries usually consist of a jelly roll and safety components. The jelly roll is sandwiched by a positive electrode (NCA: Nickel Cobalt Aluminum Oxide (LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2)), a separator (Polyolefin), and a negative electrode (Graphite) as shown in Fig. 1a. The electrolyte used in Panasonic batteries typically consists of a lithium salt (LiPF6) in a mixture of organic carbonates, such as ethyl carbonate and ethyl methyl carbonate in a 1:1 ratio, which can be vaporized in ambient air upon leakage [30]. The separator in the cell is made of polyolefin to prevent short circuits, and the positive and negative current collectors consist of aluminum and copper, respectively. During discharging, the below electrochemical reactions occur [31]:

In the negative electrode:
(1)

The electrochemical and thermal performance of NCR18650BD was tested under different C rates using a battery tester (NEWARE, BTS-4000) [29]. Five negative temperature coefficient (NTC) thermistors were attached on the outer surface of the battery to measure the surface temperature with uncertainty of ±0.2 °C. During testing, the battery was initially operated under the ambient temperature 24 °C and the cut-off voltage was set to be 4.2 V. Once completely charged, the battery was discharged at a constant C rate of 0.5, 1, or 2C. In addition, a NCR18650B battery was tested by the E-load of a Fuel Cell test system (Scribner 850e) at two different rates (0.3 and 0.9 C, respectively) and under the room temperature 25 °C. The geometrical and operating parameters of the Li-ion batteries are listed in Table 1 .

To resolve the nonlinear couplings between thermal and electrochemical dynamics, a multi-physics battery model based on finite element method, consisting of a negative tab, mandrel, outer can, active material, and positive tab, was developed. Fig. 1(c) shows the computational domain of the whole cell in the numerical platform AutoLion™. Notably, the developed model was based on the theories of porous electrode and concentrated solution [32], a couple of assumptions were made as below:
1)
Electrolyte is a concentrated binary solution.
2)
Gas formation and side reactions are ignored.
3)
Electrochemical kinetics is approximated by the Butler-Volmer (B-V) equation.
4)
Diffusion drives Li-ion transport in the active material.
5)
Active electrode materials are made up of spherical particles with a uniform size.
6)
Volume change during operation is neglected.
7)
Ion transport in the electrolyte is driven by diffusion and migration.
8)
Interfacial chemical/electrical equilibrium is held at the electrode/electrolyte interface.

The kinetics of the anode and cathode electrochemical reactions (Eqs. (1) and (2)) are approximated by the Butler–Volmer equation:
(4)

In general, the above battery model framework is similar to that in Li-air battery [34–36] and flow battery [37–39], in terms of transport equations and electrochemical kinetics description.

The initial conditions are assumed uniform for the main quantities:
(15)
c e
=
c e 0
,
c s
=
c s 0
a n d
T =
T 0

The Neumann boundary condition specifies zero gradient at boundaries for Li+ concentration and charge potentials as below:
(16)
∂
c
L i
∂ n
= 0 ,
∂
∅ s
∂ n

a n d
∂
∅ e
∂ n
= 0

The heat convection across the battery surface was considered by applying the following boundary condition:
(17)
− k
∂ T
∂ n
= h
(
T −
T a
)
where h (W m-2 K-1) is the heat transfer coefficient of the surface cooling and
T a
(K) the coolant temperature or the ambient temperature if cooled by ambient air.

The governing equations and boundary conditions, along with the parameters used for the model development and materials properties, are presented in Table 2 . These parameters are either directly measured or from literatures. This software is using finite element method with the time step size of 5. The maximum iteration per time step is 100 and the convergence criteria is 1 × 10-8. The electrical meshing consists of number of elements in length and height direction are 4 and 2, respectively, and the number of elements in circumference in the thermal meshing is 15 as shown in Fig. 1(c). The calculations were performed on a standard PC equipped with an Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-6700K CPU @ 4.00 GHz and 16 GB RAM.

The developed multi-physics model was employed to generate massive data under various operating conditions. a dataset containing the battery capacity, voltage, state of charge (SOC), and temperature were generated using the physics-based model under a wide range of working conditions, including the C rate, coolant or ambient temperature, heat transfer coefficient of surface cooling, and material anisotropic thermal conductivities along the axial and in-plane directions, as shown in Table 3 . The data was prepared for training a LSTM-CNN to predict the curves of cell voltage versus capacity, as well as the relation between cell temperature and capacity. Similar methods have been employed in recent studies of data-driven surrogate modeling [40,41,42]. Note that 3D physical modeling, even validated against a few experimental results, does not guarantee all generated data are reliable and accurate due to numerical errors or divergence. These errors or divergence data can greatly reduce machine learning accuracy. Thus, in-depth examination of the data is necessary before they are used to train ML models, as what has been done by this study.

A schematic of data-driven ML was shown in Fig. 1(d) to highlight the synergies between the multi-physics battery model and data-driven LSTM-CNN towards the accurate prediction of battery performance. The input data first goes through a 1-D convolutional layer consisting of 6 filters of size 4 which captures the spatial features of the voltage-capacity and temperature-capacity curves under various operating conditions. These features are then processed by an LSTM network with a hidden size 256 to analyze and predict battery voltage and temperature based on different discharge currents, heat transfer coefficients, ambient temperatures, and thermal conductivities. The details of the LSTM-CNN architecture and related training procedures are described in Part II [43].

A total number of 1686 data were utilized with 80 % for training and 20 % for testing. When training LSTM-CNN model, it is crucial for all training data to have a consistent length. The padding technique is used for LSTM-CNN data generation, in which padding values of 0 are added to the beginning or the end of short sequences to achieve the required length, e.g. from the maximum charge voltage 4.2 V to the minimum discharge voltage 2.5 V. In addition, an aligning technique is applied to ensure all input data sequences commence from the same point to maintain temporal consistency.

The purpose of the numerical studies in this section was thoroughly validating the developed battery model by comparing the predicted battery performance against experimental curves and parametric studies of the effects of various material and operating conditions on the battery electrochemical and thermal behaviors.

Fig. 2 depicts the comparison between the multi-physics battery model and experimental curves [29] under 3 discharge C rates, i.e., 0.5, 1 and 2 C, respectively. Overall, the model exhibits reasonable deviations of 3.5 % and 7 % regarding battery voltage and surface temperature, respectively. Specifically, the predicted curves follow reasonable trends as experimental ones, particularly a significant drop observed near 3 V. Battery temperatures increase during discharging at different paces due to Joule heating as expected. Compared to case 0.5 C, case 2 C tend to have higher surface temperature 42 °C because the released heat was not sufficiently removed by the surface cooling. Importantly, the predicted temperature is a relevant indicator to tell the battery safety state by comparing it against the safety threshold (e.g., 50 °C).

Fig. 3 further presents the model validation against another set of experimental curves from Panasonic NCR18650B at two different discharge rates. Good alignment was observed again proved by an averaged error 4.9 % in case 0.3 C. The profiles of cell voltage versus capacity reasonably fit experimental data, proving the accuracy of the developed model. The model validation was consolidated by analyzing another case 0.9 C which started discharge from a partial charge state SOC 80 %, proving that the developed model is capable of predicting battery performance at arbitrary SOC conditions.

Local temperature distribution is relevant for battery safety monitoring but challenging to obtain if only relying on experimental tools. In contrast, it is convenient for multi-physics battery model to reveal local temperature distribution. For instance, Fig. 4 shows the spatial temperature distribution for a battery operating under 2 C discharging rate, anisotropic thermal conductivities of 1&10 W m-1K-1, h = 20 W m-2K-1 and Tamb = 22 °C. The entire temperature field reasonably distributes by showing high temperature at the core and low temperature around the battery surface, as expected from heat transfer laws of conduction and convection. Notably, maximum battery temperature, remaining one of main concerns for the thermal battery safety, has been analyzed from Fig. 4. It is found that the center region tends to exhibit hot area, around 2 °C higher than the battery surface and 5 °C than the battery top.

Li-ion batteries are typically composed of composite materials and thus exhibit complex anisotropic thermal conduction. In the study, we investigated the effects of anisotropic thermal conduction for batteries by assigning different axial and radial thermal conductivity in the battery jelly roll. Fig. 5 presents the effects of different pairs of thermal conductivities (0.1&1 W m-1K-1 and 9&90 W m-1K-1) on the local temperature distribution. It is shown that low thermal conductivity results in much large temperature gradient and a high maximum temperature 70 °C, despite the surface temperature remains around safety threshold 50 °C. This large temperature gradient is explained by Fourier's law which indicates high thermal resistance for materials with low thermal conductivity. The comparison between two pairs conductivities also indicate that low local thermal conductivity can result in high local temperature and possibly trigger thermal runaway. Therefore, improving local thermal conductivity is potential to mitigate local high temperature of the battery. Fig. 5b exactly shows applying high thermal conductivity materials significantly reduced the maximum temperature to 44 °C.

Th effects of different thermal conductivities on battery performance are further displayed in Fig. 6 where batteries were simulated in three cases, i.e., k= 0.1&1, 1&10 and 9, 90 W m-1K-1, under 2C rate, h = 20 W m-1K-1and T amb = 22 °C. Minor battery performance difference was observed in the majority of range except the end of discharging. This is because the initial battery heat generation (100 %−30 % SOC) increases slowly but both reversible and irreversible heating increase quickly at the end of discharging (∼30 % to 0 % SOC) [44], scaling the temperature discrepancy among the different conductivities and hence their battery voltage difference. Additionally, the discrepancy is attributed to the different core temperature which improve kinetics and charge transport when high, reducing voltage loss and favor battery efficiency [45].

Considering of threshold temperature for battery safety, the maximum temperature is relevant to decide achievable capacity. As shown in Fig. 6, low thermal conductivity results in fast growing of temperature. The cut-off capacity values for the case with the lowest thermal conductivity under safety threshold temperature 50 °C and 60 °C are 1300 and 1800 mAh, respectively, indicating low thermal conductivity significantly limits the achievable capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of cooling heat transfer coefficient
ce:section.ce:para: Four sets of heat transfer coefficients h were studied to investigate the effects of cooling convection on the performance of battery Panasonic NCR18650BD operating 2C discharging rate. The value of h ranges from 1 to 80 W m-2K-1 to mimic different cooling scenarios. As shown in Fig. 7 , the battery temperatures reasonably increase as time going due to the Joule and reaction heats during discharging. However, the battery performed differently when free and force convection were applied, respectively. For natural convection cooling (<30 W m-2K-1), the battery had maximum temperatures exceeding safe thermal temperature threshold 50 °C or 60 °C and thus had to cut off discharging around 1200 or 1800 mAh. It proves that increasing h to 10 W m-2K-1 has limited improvement of achievable capacity (1700 mAh for 50 °C). However, for force convection cooling, the achievable capacity was significantly improved to even the full theoretical capacity.

The ambient temperature is another crucial parameter in convection cooling. The normal room temperature is usually around 20 °C, while some of the most extreme temperatures ever recorded globally can reach as high as 60 °C. Fig. 8 shows the maximum battery temperature and capacity at 2 C discharging rate and three ambient temperatures, i.e., 20, 40 and 60 °C. It is seen that the battery maximum temperature increases more rapidly at the beginning for case 40 and 60 °C than initial temperature of 20 °C, because both the waste heat and ambient (through the cooling flow) warm up battery. The temperature increasement slows down after 20 °C when the cooling performance is triggered. Higher ambient temperature contributes to higher battery voltage due to the increased battery temperature which improves both transport and kinetic properties. For case 60 °C, the battery temperature remains below 50 °C and 60 °C till the battery was discharged 600 mAh and1000 mAh, respectively, less than one quarter or half of the original capacity without any thermal limit (about 2700 mAh). Case 60 °C exactly verified the huge capacity loss to maintain battery safety under extremely hot climate conditions. The achievable capacity in case 40 °C was significantly higher than extreme hot case, demonstrating 1200 mAh and 2200 mAh under threshold temperature 50 and 60 °C, respectively. Case 20 °C allowed the battery discharge (1700 mAh) the full capacity with a very safe maximum temperature 40 °C towards safety temperature threshold.

This study delivered one of imperative steps towards cost-effective data-driven battery informatics. A multi-physics battery model was developed to numerically predict battery performance by resolving a set of physical and electrochemical governing equations. An in-depth examination of the data was performed, which aligned well with two sets of different experimental curves with maximum error less than 7 % regarding the curves cell voltage versus capacity. An in-depth parametric study was conducted to further verify the effects of different thermal conductivities, heat transfer coefficients and ambient temperatures on battery performance. It was found that battery performance is significantly sensitive to low thermal conductivity which caused local high temperature (hot spot) and possibly triggered thermal runaway and undermined battery safety. Moreover, high cooling parameters, i.e., heat transfer coefficients, played an effective role in enhancing achievable battery capacity by slowing down the increasement of maximum battery temperature (hot spot) to the threshold safety temperature. Lastly, it was verified that batteries behaved ‘vulnerable’ when operating under extreme hot ambient temperature (60 °C) due to too fast local temperature growing and lost nearly half theoretical capacity under the safe operation. The thorough validation and analysis of the developed multi-physics battery model proves feasibility and reliability by applying numerical data for data development of battery informatics.

Yiheng Pang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhiqiang Niu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Yiheng Pang and Yun Wang thanks UCI CORCLR award for support.
